item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Me being a Hoosier myself, this made me feel weird and funny. Yes, Indiana is known for the Limestone business, but I loved this. Good depictions. You have a +1 coming your way.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Okay, so I see two potential links o squeeze in here. The first, is turning the mention of Site-81 into an inline link to that Site Dossier. The second, is turning the mention of the quarry in the third addenda into a crosslink to SCP-2951, since both are kaktus skips involving companies searching for something within the limestone quarries of Indiana. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Observational Site 81-2 has been established thirty km from SCP-2812-1 along the SR ██ exit on US ██, currently posing as an abandoned used-car dealership. This site is to be staffed with no fewer than three Foundation agents posing as local law enforcement, and two staff researchers. Due to the nature of SCP-2812-1, and the effect it has on human behavior and local architecture and engineering, the on-site staff is to be rotated out once every four weeks, and exiting staff will undergo routine physical and mental health examinations.

To this:

Observational Site 81-2 has been established thirty km from SCP-2812-1 along the SR ██ exit on US ██, currently posing as an abandoned used-car dealership. This site is to be staffed with no fewer than three Foundation agents posing as local law enforcement, and two staff researchers. Due to the nature of SCP-2812-1, and the effect it has on human behavior and local architecture and engineering, the on-site staff is to be rotated out once every four weeks, and exiting staff will undergo routine physical and mental health examinations.

Changing this:

You know good and well that my men cleared for that quarry months ago. I don't know where you get off thinking that you can simply roll in and set up shop without going through all the necessary avenues, but I won't stand for it. I don't know what you're finding in there, but it is by right mine and I demand you release it back to me. That property has been in my family for generations, and as soon as I get a hold of another copy of the title to the land that I know you had stolen from me I will march down to Stack Rock with the sheriff and load you into the squad car myself. By the time I'm finished with you, I'll have scorched the earth so thoroughly in ███████ that your grandchildren will feel the heat.

To this:

You know good and well that my men cleared for that quarry months ago. I don't know where you get off thinking that you can simply roll in and set up shop without going through all the necessary avenues, but I won't stand for it. I don't know what you're finding in there, but it is by right mine and I demand you release it back to me. That property has been in my family for generations, and as soon as I get a hold of another copy of the title to the land that I know you had stolen from me I will march down to Stack Rock with the sheriff and load you into the squad car myself. By the time I'm finished with you, I'll have scorched the earth so thoroughly in ███████ that your grandchildren will feel the heat.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-2812-2 instances have shown to be hostile towards humans, stalking individuals who have remained in ███████, or including Foundation agents. "

I fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rescue efforts into the hospital have been unsuccessful.

Can you expand on this line? The SCP-2812-2 instances sound potentially deadly but only to unarmed civilians since they can be driven off with "light arms fire". A modestly equipped rescue effort with a few handguns should be enough to rescue the people in the hospital.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Well, if you must know... {omg spoilers}
- Recollapse Collapsible

It isn't because he owned the town. Look at it like this; Hedgewater and his company operate out of a town near the town where the record player is. He'd been there for a while before Indiana Limestone came in and set up shop, and started pumping out limestone (and other things) from the quarry that Hedgewater owned. Hedgewater wasn't thrilled about this, so he decided to obliterate the town that Indiana Limestone and all of its workers were situated in. Something happened to the Indiana Limestone guys, because they never turned the record player on, but that's best left to speculation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is seriously fantastic. Spooky as hell, air of mystery, understated. How did I miss this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get why Hedgewater would drive out everyone in the town with his device. Is it because the entire town his property and his property alone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea of a thing which makes you want to get the hell out of town, and how nonlethal yet utterly terrifying that is, but I find myself utterly ambivalent towards Hedgewater, mildly irritated by the woman with cancer which didn't really add anything for me and felt like an attempt to get some sympathy, and actively rolling my eyes at the creepy speaker drones who can teleport and who attack you for reasons.

That said, it's well-written for a first, so I no - vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Up vote. As someone who is currently working on their first SCP, it's nice to see a first be well recieved.

One thing that's probably not a huge deal, but really bugs me, is that the area of effect is blacked out. That seems like pretty important information that wouldn't be blacked out, and having it that way doesn't really add to the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I respect your opinion, I also didn't say everybody was gone, or that those who had left all left at the same time.

Edit: I don't know if that sounds like a cop out, but I was just explaining that I had a reason for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't the most amazing skip I've ever read, but it's excellent written, which is a must for a long article, and keeps getting better as it goes. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kalinin words it better than I could have. Unlike him, I was unfazed by the technical things in his line-by-line, but the article completely lost me at "Subjects in the town of ███████ universally believe that they have to "get out of town" and not return."

Aside from his complaint of inserting prose into quotes in a clinical document (that being a major peeve of mine as well), overall this touches on another big thing that I strongly dislike—-homogeny.

Compulsion is mental/psychological. Not all people are identical, not just in terms of psyche but in actual brain chemistry and structure sometimes. If you poke a hole in the exact same spot in 100 people's brains, you're not going to have 100 identical psychological results (other than maybe 100 severe migraines or death by hole in the brain).

Having everyone just universally go "Ew I must leave" comes across as completely artificial and in no way realistic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ line-by-line
- Hide text

Observational Site 81-2 has been established thirty (30) km from SCP-2812-1

This isn't a mistake, per se, but the site has generally gotten away from the "number in parentheses" thing, as it's not really necessary.

Fences were initially constructed around the business plaza where SCP-2812-1 is located, but have since fallen into disrepair. The door to the building housing SCP-2812-1 is locked.

The issues with extraneous stuff in this piece begin here. We don't really need to know about the fences, and of course the door is locked.

SCP-2812-1 is a 1930's style record player

I feel like there's a better way to describe this. This is a step above "SCP-2812 is an old-timey record player".

The player is made up almost entirely of wood, brass, and glass, with other smaller fixtures included (although nothing unusual of a record player of the time).

Unless that's important to the narrative somehow, I don't think this part is necessary.

The runtime of the track is 5:14, after which there is ten (10) second pause, and then the track repeats itself.

Not really necessary to include this.

SCP-2812-1 does not appear to be connected to any visible power source.

You would think that the Foundation could have at least established whether this thing is plugged in or not. "Does not appear" sounds silly in that context, it is or it isn't. Also, the "plays even when it's not plugged in" thing doesn't really seem necessary, and thus falls into "overused trope" territory.

The primary anomalous effect of SCP-2812-1 is a powerful physical and psychological compulsion apparent in all individuals within SCP-2812-1's range of effect.

I find that "thing what make you do stuff" fails to resonate on any level for me about 99% of the time. There's no qualities about the item itself that would naturally cause any sort of reaction and therefore put force into a narrative, it's just straight-up mind control. I guess mind control is unsettling on some level by itself, but the ramifications of that have been explored in far better detail, here and elsewhere.

Subjects in the town of ███████ universally believe that they have to "get out of town" and not return.

Either everyone in the town literally says "I must get out of town", which would be exceedingly silly, or there's a better way to say this in a clinical document. "universally believe that they must relocate" or something similar would work far better.

usually ending in the individuals (and, if applicable, their families) moving away from ███████ completely, regardless of the state of their affairs.

Wait, wouldn't the individual's family members be affected by the skip independently?

Once they have sufficiently "gotten away" from the town and SCP-2812-1

See comments above about the "get out of town" thing.

When asked about their actions, most will claim that they "just couldn't take it any longer"

And again.

Of the 13.4k residents

That seems like an odd abbreviation choice. I personally think "13,400" would look better here.

it is estimated that only 190 remain as of 20██

I'm confused as to why any people would remain, given the skip's effects.

The compulsion to get away from SCP-2812-1 seemingly increases as individuals approach the "epicenter" of the effect.

You may want to adjust the first part of the description to account for this, because I started reading this as the skip affecting all people within its radius (which is what you actually write in that part) and then run into this.

Parts of the town will begin to show signs of being "run-down"

This is a recurring thing with this article. You can't just put prose in quotes and then drop it in a clinical document. The quotes don't suddenly make phrases take on specific meanings such as would be necessary to describe something from a scientific viewpoint.

The effects of SCP-2812-1 seem to be enhanced by SCP-2812-2, and at close range have resulted in severe cerebral hemorrhaging in ██% of those affected.

I don't get why the percentage needs to be redacted here. I know that's a common practice in older articles, but I don't see the actual reason behind it now.

However, SCP-2812-2 instances are not immaterial; they can be driven off with light arms fire, and those struck will disappear and reappear at a later time.

I'm not sure why the default supposition is that the speaker monsters are ghosts.

Fortunately, due to the duration in which SCP-2812-1 has been left "on" (currently estimated at █ years), very few residents of the town remain as prey.

"Fortunately" is poor tone here. This is a clinical document describing an anomalous phenomenon. I'm also unsure why the term "prey" is used here; up to this point the monsters are just killing people for reasons. Do they eat the people or somehow gain some benefit from it? Because you haven't said anything to this point about it.

Interview 2812 Perry-A

This didn't feel natural at all. She seems pretty clear-headed for supposedly being a lung cancer patient that's needed to be hospitalized for weeks, let alone not receiving adequate medical care. She also seems fairly comfortable with the fact that a strange robot has just showed up to interview her. Also, and this may merely anecdotal evidence, I haven't met a ton of people in the real estate profession that causally drop the term "motherfucker" into conversation.

and as soon as I get a hold of another copy of the title to the land that I know you had stolen from me I will

He bolded text in a personal letter?

Foundation agents on a routine patrol of the near-epicenter region of SCP-2812-1's range of effect reported that the music playing from SCP-2812-1

I thought you said that people can't reliably go in to investigate this?

As a result of Event 2812-Epsilon, the effective range of SCP-2812-1 has been increased to ██ km,

Given the lack of understanding of the pseudo-scientific principles at work here, I have a hard time believing that the Foundation conclusively proved that the event itself caused the range to increase. The best it seems that could have been done was to note that the two events coincided.

This one doesn't work for me. I find that there's systemic issues in the execution, for starters, which makes this kind of difficult to get through. There's this weird dichotomy of overly-detailed scientific description that instead of lending an air of authenticity merely lengthens the word count, and lapses into prose that are ineffectively handwaved by adding quotes around them.

I also feel like I'm supposed to find this skip spooky, which, hey, great, I love spooky. The problem is that the tools you're using aren't really up to the job, in my opinion. The record player feels like something out of a stock internet creepypasta, with the working even when not plugged in thing, and I just can't take the monsters you have seriously at all. The "blasting old timey music at you with mouth speakers until you brain damage to death" thing is just too goofy for me, and reminds me of Homer Simpson talking about dogs that shoot bees out of their mouth when they bark at you more than anything else.

I do think that "death of the small town" has some narrative force and could be adapted into a workable article. Unfortunately, I don't think this is that article at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you did a good job with them, it isn't easy to have scarequotes that don't detract from the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good catch, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The plural of phenomenon is phenomena

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a goddamn amazing first work.
Perhaps it's just my having been raised in the Midwest, seeing so many small towns rot from the inside, so many car trips where I was told "it used to be a lot nicer, back in the day," but this resonated very strongly with me.
A well deserved +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Repeating everything I said in the drafts forum: just an awesome specimen of an SCP, for many of the reasons Roget lists above; there's a definite atmospheric feel to it, and even if it isn't "gah field of doooooom" exactly, it is there, and the subtlety is all the more impressive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought about what you were saying, and although I really do value your opinion, I'm not sure that I agree. The thing is, at their heart, these articles are still clinical reports. I imagine the Foundation would care less about the squabbles of two men who died decades ago, and more about the humanoid jukeboxes that can teleport and kill people. With that in mind, I don't see why they wouldn't want the bulk of the detail to come in the form of description and containment information. Now, I understand from an entertainment standpoint, the longer description might not be captivating, and it may feel like there's some being left out when it comes to the "plot", but that's how it would be, realistically. They wouldn't have access to much of "the story", but would have plenty of information about the thing itself.

I may be way off base here, but that was just my line of thinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hmm

I'm a neutral vote on the verge of an upvote. The problems which I mentioned in chat, while mitigated, are still present. I feel that there is still some fluff that can be cut down-extensive descriptions of the -2 instances tracking "prey," which doesn't really contribute anything because the creepy part is the stupid speaker cone in their mouths, pretty long descriptions of the psychological compulsion, etc. Even if you want to keep those details, you can definitely tighten up the description section by just cutting out extraneous stuff, like, "it's not connected to a power source."

So then, we get to the end, where we get the backstory behind this thing. It's a decent backstory, but I still feel that in this article, it's underdeveloped. One of the challenges of interweaving narrative into an article is that a narrative needs characters. Your narrative is pretty plot-heavy, but the development of the characters goes only so far as "pissed that his land got jacked," and Matherson is just…"polite person with weird undertones."

I think this is boiling down to a matter of priorities in your writing. You are still spending a lot of time on your basic idea…essentially the entirety of your fairly long description center…but it's hard for me to upvote on that alone. That being the case, I have to read through all of your description to get to the morsels of a story that you've left at the bottom. You want your basic idea to be conveyed quickly and to the point, then you can put detail into your narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And honestly, a lot of that stemmed from me feeling like I was repeating myself if I tried to describe everything clinically. I ended up with that nagging feeling you get when you use a word too much, and went back to try and readjust things to keep it interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. It really gave me the atmosphere of driving through some small, mid-state town where the air is muggy, the water doesn't taste right, and all you want to do is get out. I thought the scare quotes could maybe be reduced, but they never particularly bothered me. I just know other people feel an incredible, terrible rage upon seeing a single quotation mark.

item.title: First go around
item.content:encoded:
Well, here we go. Thanks again for everybody who read through this and gave advice/critiques/comments on the forums/chat. It's been a blast!

Edit: future kaktus here. Just pointing out that you all could've stopped me here. You had your chance. You're all complicit now.

Edit2: future kaktus here again. Still nothing.

Edit3: 2020 kaktus here reporting live from the pandemic. all images in this article were originally taken by me, djkaktus, and are released under Creative Commons.
